# Just Write, the free writing space with no delete button

JustWrite is a free online writing app to practice free writing, a way to overcome writer's block.

Write anything. Don't edit or delete what you wrote (you can't).  
Just keep writing.

  

Start

  

🖥️📱 Desktop and mobile apps available (Win, Mac, Android)

results.title: 6 Best Scrivener Alternatives for Writers (+ The Only One That Matters)
results.content:
Check Out Atticus Today!

(And check out our in-depth Atticus review for more info!)

## 2. iA Writer – You Just Want Simpler and Cheaper

I find iA Writer to be the easiest, and cleanest word processor of the list. Honestly, think of it like the Honda Civic of tools. It has a simple interface and really helps you to focus. The focus mode feature highlights only one sentence at a time, so even your other words don't distract you! Now, some of you might not enjoy that and it took me a bit to get used to.

I personally didn’t choose to keep iA Writer because I don’t need focus mode for my writing, prefer to have more features, and needed something that helped to format my book. Why not have one tool you need to learn and to run all your book writing right? [...] ### SmartEdit Writer

Formerly Atomic Scribbler, SmartEdit Writer is a free tool designed to help you organize and edit your manuscript. You can store links and images in the research section, which is easy to access as you write. You can also write scene-by-scene, dragging and dropping them wherever you please. Plus, this writing software helps you locate overused words and phrases, along with adverbs and cliches! If you work in MS Word, you'll feel right at home in this app.

SmartEdit Writer Price: Free

### Dabble Writer

Created in 2017 by Jacob Wright, Dabble Writer claims it's “Scrivener without the learning curve.” There's a lot to like about it, from its clean interface to its focus mode, but it's more expensive than I'd like.

## Conclusion [...] Microsoft Word Price: $159.99 standalone or $6.99 a month with Microsoft 365. You can also use Microsoft Office Online for free.

### Quoll Writer

Quoll Writer is an open-source software that's a viable alternative to Scrivener. It works both as a plotting tool and a word processor, much like Scrivener does. Plus, it has a full-screen mode for those who don't want to be distracted when they're writing. But perhaps the best thing about it is that it's free. However, it is severely limited in its capability and hasn't updated in a while. Complaints have been made online about some glitches as well.

### SmartEdit Writer

results.title: I tested 22 writing tools—yeah, almost all of them—so you don't have ...
results.content:
## Sudowrite

Image 24

Sudowrite is all about AI. That’s the core. It’s built to help you brainstorm, generate content, expand scenes, and more. It’s packed with features. There’s even a canvas where you can drop notes and images. I like that. It lets you visualize your mess a bit.

But the writing experience is not for me. The interface is too busy, too many popups, too many things happening. You can collapse the UI, which helps. But I’m just not someone who can write surrounded by that much distraction.

Still, if you’re someone who wants AI for feedback on your work, it’s worth a look. Rating: 6.5/10

  

## AutoCrit

Image 25 [...] ## Novelize

Image 23

Novelize has been around forever, but I’ve never clicked with it. It’s functional, sure—you can write chapters and scenes, it organizes your story. But it feels dated. The gray interface, the washed-out text, and then there’s this bright orange that just takes over everything. It’s like they’re trying to make sure their brand is the loudest thing on the screen.

At $9/month, I expected something more immersive. The writing experience doesn’t feel good, and I don’t enjoy being in it. There are better tools for the same price. Rating: 5.5/10

  

## Sudowrite

Image 24 [...] ## Reedsy Studio

Image 28

Reedsy Studio is amazing for one thing: turning your finished manuscript into something print-ready. I don’t use it to write, but when I’ve got a draft done, I drop it in here and use it to generate an EPUB or a print-ready PDF.

The export quality is great and the layout just works. It’s free, which is incredible. The only problem? It crashes if you try to do too much at once or edit inside it. But for final formatting? I love it. Rating: 8/10

  

## Bear / UpNote

Bear is Mac-only, so I’ve not been able to use it (I only have Windows). UpNote looks like the Windows alternative and I’ve used it quite a bit.

Image 29

It’s gorgeous—clean writing experience, visual folders, thumbnail previews when you drop in images.

Image 30

results.title: EVERY WRITING SOFTWARE OPTION FOR WRITERS - YouTube
results.content: just how easy it is. Yeah, on the website it says that you can explore with drafts, so you don't create like a different draft for every chapter or anything. Ulysses also doesn't use any generative AI and is very protective against AI and so none of the text that you use in Ulysses will be in any way used to train or given to AIs in any way. Like it's very, very safe and protected. Right now Ulysses is completely free. I'm sure they will roll out some paid pricing tiers in the future, but right now it's totally free. So, if you're interested in going and checking it out, that is also another alternative to novelist. It allows you to do more. It's not quite as simple, but it also can be. A lot of these programs are only as complicated as you make them. So, the next program on this list is [...] probably not one that I would start with just when you are seeing whether or not like you want that kind of structure. But if you know already know that you're a plotter and you like being able to track things like that or you want a little bit more features support, it's a really great one to try out before you make the investment in something more expensive. So Novlr is another option. I've heard really great things about Novlr. I don't really know anything about it, but you can create projects and write in Novlr. It is structured kind of similarly to the other word processors that we've been talking about. So it's much more minimalist. It doesn't have as many like bells and whistles, so it's more intuitive and easy to follow. A lot of people get really overwhelmed if a program has too [...] little nodes in your plot grid that correspond to this particular chapter. So, you can keep track of everything that needs to happen in this chapter in order for your outline to work. So, I think this is incredibly helpful. I absolutely love Dabble for this. Dabble saves your work locally and also to the cloud. And so, it makes it really easy like if you want to sync between your devices or if you want to write on your phone, you can just log in. You have to write in their like online word processor, but you can access all of your projects offline and then they will be saved locally and then backed up to the cloud as soon as you connect to Wi-Fi. You can also put Dabble in dark mode and like change how it actually looks if you like prefer writing in dark mode. Dabble is the most expensive
